system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 13: Three consecutive eighth-note triplets

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 13 — Three consecutive eighth-note triplets

_Pages 61-63._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## consecutive triplets review

> To review the material pertaining to consecutive triplets in Chapters 9-13, try playing broken

**Significance (p. 61).** Internalizing triplet groupings via broken-solo application.

## sixteenth notes from eighth notes

> To practice playing consecutive sixteenth notes take the material presented in Chapters 3-8 on
> consecutive eighth notes and convert them to sixteenth-note exercises by doubling up. For

**Significance (p. 62).** Core conversion rule extending eighth-note vocabulary into double-time sixteenth-note playing.

## 2/4 to 4/4 repetition rule

> The exercises are then all in 2/4 time so you can repeat the 2/4 phrase twice to make a 4/4 bar.

**Significance (p. 62).** How sixteenth-note exercises fit into 4/4 jazz contexts.

## double-time playing value

> This way of practicing sixteenth notes is very valuable for double-time

**Significance (p. 62).** Connects method to double-time improvisation.

## hemiola overview

> This chapter presents 3/4 rhythms to be played over 4/4 tunes.

**Significance (p. 63).** Defines hemiola as performance technique.

## hemiola musical benefits

> Practicing this rhythmic technique
> helps the improviser to become independent of bar lines and makes one’s playing more horizontal.
> Playing 3/4 rhythms over 4/4 tunes insures that the player is beginning phrases from various
> beats within the bar and not starting every line on only beat one. Practicing this technique opens a
> window in the mind that hears bigger spaces of time, like four-bar phrases or eight-bar phrases, or
> sixteen bars.

**Significance (p. 63).** Full rationale: rhythmic independence, phrase variety, formal awareness, harmonic anticipation.

## hemiola structure rule

> It
> takes four bars of 3/4 to equal three bars of 4/4. The 3/4 rhythm starts again on the first beat after
> it has been played through three bars of 4/4.

**Significance (p. 63).** Precise arithmetic rule for hemiola resolution.

## hemiola practice method

> Try to practice these rhythms with a play along and also without a play
> along while your right foot taps beat one and your left foot taps beat 3. Also, try singing

**Significance (p. 63).** Physical/aural method for internalizing hemiola.

## counting and awareness

> The seasoned improviser is not counting the number of eighth notes as he or she
> plays. But practicing in this way and playing these exercises does give the improviser a new

**Significance (p. 61).** Conscious practice builds subconscious awareness.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
